content:
# Exodus Chapter 17 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~43r–43v (PDF pp. 81–82); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 58–59 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_017_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_017_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_017_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_017_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — TWO-PART NARRATIVE: WATER CRISIS AND HOLY WAR:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 17 in standard prose column format across two pages. The chapter divides into two distinct narratives: the water crisis at Massah and Meribah (vv. 1–7) and the battle with Amaleq (vv. 8–16). The first episode follows the established wilderness complaint pattern (journey, need, quarrel, divine provision) but escalates to a legal register — the people riv (quarrel, contend at law) with Mosheh and nassah (test, put on trial) YHWH. The second episode introduces Yehoshua (Joshua) for the first time in the Torah and presents a unique theology of victory: Yisra'el prevails not by military prowess but by Mosheh's raised hands holding the matteh ha-Elohim (staff of God). The chapter concludes with the naming of an altar — YHWH Nissi ("YHWH is my banner") — and a cryptic oath of perpetual war against Amaleq. The Vaticanus text (pp. 58–59) shows the LXX rendering in continuous uncial script; the transition from chapter 16 to 17 is visible on page 58, with chapter 17 continuing onto page 59.

---

## Water from the Rock at Massah and Meribah (vv. 1–7)

**1** And all the congregation ['adat] of the sons [benei] of Yisra'el journeyed [vayis'u] from the midbar [wilderness] of Sin, by their journeyings [le-mas'eihem], upon the mouth of [al-pi] YHWH, and they encamped [vayakhanu] at Refidim; and there was no [ve-ein] mayim [water] for the people [la-am] to drink [lishtot].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּסְעוּ כָּל־עֲדַת בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל מִמִּדְבַּר־סִין לְמַסְעֵיהֶם עַל־פִּי יְהוָה וַיַּחֲנוּ בִּרְפִידִם וְאֵין מַיִם לִשְׁתֹּת הָעָם (vayis'u kol-adat benei-Yisra'el mi-midbar-Sin le-mas'eihem al-pi YHWH vayakhanu bi-Rfidim ve-ein mayim lishtot ha-am, "and all the congregation of the sons of Yisra'el journeyed from the wilderness of Sin, by their journeyings, upon the mouth of YHWH, and they encamped at Refidim; and there was no water for the people to drink"). **Key terms:** לְמַסְעֵיהֶם (le-mas'eihem) = "by their journeyings / according to their stages" — from מַסָּע (massa, "journey, stage of a journey"), from נסע (nasa, "to pull up [tent pegs], set out, journey"). The plural with possessive suffix indicates the series of encampments prescribed by YHWH. עַל־פִּי יְהוָה (al-pi YHWH) = "upon the mouth of YHWH" — literally "by the mouth of YHWH," meaning at YHWH's command (cf. Num 33:2). Every stage of the journey is directed by divine word. רְפִידִם (Refidim) = the name of the encampment, of uncertain etymology; some connect it to רפד (rafad, "to spread out, support"), suggesting a "place of rest/support" — ironic given that there is no water. The phrase וְאֵין מַיִם (ve-ein mayim) = "and there was no water" — אֵין (ein) = the particle of non-existence, "there is not." The crisis is immediate and existential: no water in the desert. LXX: καὶ ἀπῆρεν πᾶσα συναγωγὴ υἱῶν Ισραηλ ἐκ τῆς ἐρήμου Σιν κατὰ παρεμβολὰς αὐτῶν διὰ ῥήματος κυρίου καὶ παρενέβαλον ἐν Ραφιδιν· οὐκ ἦν δὲ ὕδωρ τῷ λαῷ πιεῖν ("and all the synagogue of the sons of Israel departed from the wilderness of Sin according to their encampments, by the word of the Lord, and they encamped at Raphidin; and there was no water for the people to drink") — κατὰ παρεμβολάς ("according to encampments") for le-mas'eihem; διὰ ῥήματος κυρίου ("by the word of the Lord") for al-pi YHWH.

**2** And the people quarreled [vayarev] with Mosheh, and they said: "Give [tenu] us mayim [water] and let us drink [ve-nishteh]!" And Mosheh said to them: "Why [mah] do you quarrel [terivun] with me? Why [mah] do you test [tenassun] YHWH?"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיָּרֶב הָעָם עִם־מֹשֶׁה וַיֹּאמְרוּ תְּנוּ־לָנוּ מַיִם וְנִשְׁתֶּה וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶם מֹשֶׁה מַה־תְּרִיבוּן עִמָּדִי מַה־תְּנַסּוּן אֶת־יְהוָה (vayarev ha-am im-Mosheh vayomeru tenu-lanu mayim ve-nishteh vayomer lahem Mosheh mah-terivun immadi mah-tenassun et-YHWH, "and the people quarreled with Mosheh, and they said: 'Give us water and let us drink!' And Mosheh said to them: 'Why do you quarrel with me? Why do you test YHWH?'"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — riv and nassah, the two verbs that become place names:** The verb וַיָּרֶב (vayarev) = qal of ריב (riv, "to quarrel, contend, bring a lawsuit, strive") — this is not mere complaining (לון, lun, as in 15:24; 16:2) but a legal confrontation, a ריב (riv), a formal dispute or lawsuit. The people are putting Mosheh on trial. The noun form of this root, מְרִיבָה (Merivah), becomes the place name in v. 7. תְּרִיבוּן (terivun) = "you quarrel" — the qal imperfect 2mp with paragogic nun, giving it an emphatic or archaic quality. תְּנַסּוּן (tenassun) = "you test, you put to the test" — piel imperfect 2mp of נסה (nassah, "to test, try, prove"), also with paragogic nun. This is not the innocent testing of a hypothesis but a hostile probing: will YHWH come through, or not? It is testing YHWH's faithfulness by demanding proof. The noun form מַסָּה (Massah) = "testing, trial" becomes the second place name in v. 7. Mosheh's double question (mah-terivun...mah-tenassun) links the quarrel with him to the testing of YHWH — to contend with YHWH's appointed leader is to put YHWH Himself on trial. LXX: καὶ ἐλοιδορεῖτο ὁ λαὸς πρὸς Μωυσῆν λέγοντες· δὸς ἡμῖν ὕδωρ ἵνα πίωμεν. καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς Μωυσῆς· τί λοιδορεῖσθέ μοι; καὶ τί πειράζετε κύριον; ("and the people reviled Mosheh, saying: 'Give us water that we may drink.' And Mosheh said to them: 'Why do you revile me? And why do you test the Lord?'") — λοιδορέω ("revile, abuse, reproach") for riv — the LXX shifts from the legal/forensic sense of riv toward verbal abuse; πειράζω ("test, tempt") for nassah.

**3** And the people thirsted [vayitsma] there for the mayim [water], and the people grumbled [vayallen] against Mosheh, and he said: "Why [lammah] is this [zeh] — you brought us up [he'elitanu] from Mitsrayim to kill [le-hamit] me [oti] and my sons [ve-et-banai] and my livestock [ve-et-miqnai] by thirst [ba-tsama]?"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּצְמָא שָׁם הָעָם לַמַּיִם וַיָּלֶן הָעָם עַל־מֹשֶׁה וַיֹּאמֶר לָמָּה זֶּה הֶעֱלִיתָנוּ מִמִּצְרַיִם לְהָמִית אֹתִי וְאֶת־בָּנַי וְאֶת־מִקְנַי בַּצָּמָא (vayitsma sham ha-am la-mayim vayallen ha-am al-Mosheh vayomer lammah zeh he'elitanu mi-Mitsrayim le-hamit oti ve-et-banai ve-et-miqnai ba-tsama, "and the people thirsted there for the water, and the people grumbled against Mosheh, and he said: 'Why is this — you brought us up from Mitsrayim to kill me and my sons and my livestock by thirst?'"). **Key terms:** וַיִּצְמָא (vayitsma) = qal of צמא (tsama, "to thirst, be thirsty") — "and he/they thirsted." Physical thirst drives the complaint. וַיָּלֶן (vayallen) = hiphil of לון (lun, "to murmur, grumble") — the same verb from 15:24 and 16:2, now joined to the more aggressive riv of v. 2. The complaint escalates from grumbling to quarreling. הֶעֱלִיתָנוּ (he'elitanu) = hiphil of עלה (alah, "to go up") + 1cp suffix — "you brought us up." The accusation echoes 16:3 but is even more personal: the people speak in the singular collective voice — "to kill me and my sons and my livestock." Each family feels the threat individually. מִקְנֶה (miqneh) = "livestock, cattle, possessions" — from קנה (qanah, "to acquire, buy"). The livestock are dying too — the crisis affects everything alive. צָמָא (tsama) = "thirst" — the noun form of the verb. In chapter 16, the threat was רָעָב (ra'av, "famine/hunger"); here it is צָמָא (tsama, "thirst"). LXX: ἐδίψησεν δὲ ἐκεῖ ὁ λαὸς ὕδατι, καὶ ἐγόγγυζεν ἐκεῖ ὁ λαὸς πρὸς Μωυσῆν λέγοντες· ἵνα τί τοῦτο ἀνεβίβασας ἡμᾶς ἐξ Αἰγύπτου ἀποκτεῖναι ἡμᾶς καὶ τὰ τέκνα ἡμῶν καὶ τὰ κτήνη τῷ δίψει; ("and the people thirsted there for water, and the people murmured there against Mosheh, saying: 'Why is this — you brought us up from Egypt to kill us and our children and our cattle with thirst?'") — τέκνα ("children") for banai; κτήνη ("cattle") for miqnai; δίψος ("thirst") for tsama.

**4** And Mosheh cried out [vayits'aq] to YHWH, saying [lemor]: "What [mah] shall I do [e'eseh] for this people [la-am ha-zeh]? A little more [od me'at] and they will stone me [u-seqaluni]!"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּצְעַק מֹשֶׁה אֶל־יְהוָה לֵאמֹר מָה אֶעֱשֶׂה לָעָם הַזֶּה עוֹד מְעַט וּסְקָלֻנִי (vayits'aq Mosheh el-YHWH lemor mah e'eseh la-am ha-zeh od me'at u-seqaluni, "and Mosheh cried out to YHWH, saying: 'What shall I do for this people? A little more and they will stone me!'"). **Key terms:** וַיִּצְעַק (vayits'aq) = qal of צעק (tsa'aq, "to cry out, shout") — the same verb used of Yisra'el's outcry in Egyptian slavery (3:7, 9). Now Mosheh himself cries out, not against Pharaoh but against the people he leads. עוֹד מְעַט (od me'at) = "yet a little / a little more" — from עוֹד (od, "yet, still, again") + מְעַט (me'at, "a little, few"). The situation is on the verge of lethal violence. וּסְקָלֻנִי (u-seqaluni) = qal perfect (with waw-consecutive) of סקל (saqal, "to stone") + 1cs suffix — "and they will stone me." Stoning is the communal form of execution in ancient Israel (cf. Lev 20:2; 24:14; Num 15:35–36; Deut 17:5). That the people are near to stoning their own leader reveals the extremity of the crisis. LXX: ἐβόησεν δὲ Μωυσῆς πρὸς κύριον λέγων· τί ποιήσω τῷ λαῷ τούτῳ; ἔτι μικρὸν καὶ καταλιθοβολήσουσίν με ("and Mosheh cried to the Lord, saying: 'What shall I do for this people? Yet a little and they will stone me!'") — καταλιθοβολέω ("stone to death") for saqal — the compound verb intensifies the threat.

**5** And YHWH said to Mosheh: "Pass over [avor] before [lifnei] the people [ha-am], and take [ve-qakh] with you from the elders [mi-ziqnei] of Yisra'el; and your matteh [staff] which [asher] you struck [hikkita] the Ye'or [Nile] with it [bo] — take [qakh] in your hand [be-yadekha] and go [ve-halakhta].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה עֲבֹר לִפְנֵי הָעָם וְקַח אִתְּךָ מִזִּקְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וּמַטְּךָ אֲשֶׁר הִכִּיתָ בּוֹ אֶת־הַיְאֹר קַח בְּיָדְךָ וְהָלָכְתָּ (vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh avor lifnei ha-am ve-qakh ittkha mi-ziqnei Yisra'el u-mattekha asher hikkita bo et-ha-Ye'or qakh be-yadekha ve-halakhta, "and YHWH said to Mosheh: 'Pass over before the people, and take with you from the elders of Yisra'el; and your staff which you struck the Nile with it — take in your hand and go'"). **Key terms:** עֲבֹר (avor) = qal imperative of עבר (avar, "to pass over, cross over") — "pass over!" Mosheh is to walk ahead of the people, leading them to the place of provision. זִקְנֵי (ziqnei) = "elders of" — construct plural of זָקֵן (zaqen, "elder, old man"), from the root זקן (zaqan, "beard" / zaqen, "to be old"). The elders serve as witnesses to the miracle — YHWH wants the leadership of Yisra'el to see what He does. מַטֶּה (matteh) = "staff, rod, branch" — from נטה (natah, "to stretch out, extend, bend"). This is the same staff that struck the Nile and turned it to blood (7:17, 20), the staff of divine power. The reference to the Nile-striking links this water miracle to the Egyptian plagues — the same staff that turned water to blood will now draw water from rock. LXX: καὶ εἶπεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν· προπορεύου τοῦ λαοῦ τούτου, λαβὲ δὲ μετὰ σεαυτοῦ ἀπὸ τῶν πρεσβυτέρων τοῦ λαοῦ· καὶ τὴν ῥάβδον ἐν ᾗ ἐπάταξας τὸν ποταμόν, λαβὲ ἐν τῇ χειρί σου καὶ πορεύσῃ ("and the Lord said to Mosheh: 'Go before this people, and take with yourself from the elders of the people; and the rod with which you struck the river, take in your hand and you shall go'") — πρεσβύτερος ("elder") for zaqen; ῥάβδος ("rod, staff") for matteh; ποταμός ("river") for Ye'or.

**6** Behold me [hineni] standing [omed] before you [lefanekha] there [sham] upon [al] the rock [ha-tsur] in Khorev [Horeb]; and you shall strike [ve-hikkita] the rock [ba-tsur], and mayim [water] shall come out [ve-yatse'u] from it [mimmennu], and the people shall drink [ve-shatah]." And Mosheh did so [vaya'as ken] before the eyes [le-einei] of the elders [ziqnei] of Yisra'el.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads הִנְנִי עֹמֵד לְפָנֶיךָ שָּׁם עַל־הַצּוּר בְּחֹרֵב וְהִכִּיתָ בַצּוּר וְיָצְאוּ מִמֶּנּוּ מַיִם וְשָׁתָה הָעָם וַיַּעַשׂ כֵּן מֹשֶׁה לְעֵינֵי זִקְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (hineni omed lefanekha sham al-ha-tsur be-Khorev ve-hikkita va-tsur ve-yatse'u mimmennu mayim ve-shatah ha-am vaya'as ken Mosheh le-einei ziqnei Yisra'el, "behold me standing before you there upon the rock in Horeb; and you shall strike the rock, and water shall come out from it, and the people shall drink. And Mosheh did so before the eyes of the elders of Yisra'el"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — YHWH standing upon the rock:** The phrase הִנְנִי עֹמֵד לְפָנֶיךָ שָּׁם עַל־הַצּוּר (hineni omed lefanekha sham al-ha-tsur) = "behold me standing before you there upon the rock" — this is an extraordinary statement. YHWH Himself is standing upon the rock. When Mosheh strikes the rock, he strikes the place where YHWH stands. The water flows not from mere stone but from beneath the feet of God. The word צוּר (tsur) = "rock, cliff, crag" — from a root meaning "to be hard, sharp." צוּר is also used as a divine epithet: YHWH is "the Rock" (Deut 32:4, 15, 18, 30, 31; 2 Sam 22:2–3; Ps 18:2). The rock at Khorev is both literal geology and theological sign. חֹרֵב (Khorev / Horeb) = the mountain of God, another name for Sinai (cf. 3:1 where Mosheh first encountered the burning bush "at the mountain of God, at Khorev"). The water miracle occurs at the very mountain where YHWH will give the Torah — the journey is toward Sinai, and they are already at its threshold. וְהִכִּיתָ (ve-hikkita) = hiphil perfect (waw-consecutive) of נכה (nakhah, "to strike, smite") — "and you shall strike." The same verb used for striking the Nile (7:17, 20) and striking the Egyptians. Now the staff of judgment becomes the staff of provision. לְעֵינֵי (le-einei) = "before the eyes of, in the sight of" — the elders are eyewitnesses. This is a public miracle, not a private one. LXX: ὅδε ἐγὼ ἕστηκα πρὸ τοῦ σὲ ἐκεῖ ἐπὶ τῆς πέτρας ἐν Χωρηβ· καὶ πατάξεις τὴν πέτραν, καὶ ἐξελεύσεται ἐξ αὐτῆς ὕδωρ, καὶ πίεται ὁ λαός μου. ἐποίησεν δὲ Μωυσῆς οὕτως ἐναντίον τῶν υἱῶν Ισραηλ ("behold, I stand before you there upon the rock in Choreb; and you shall strike the rock, and water shall come out from it, and my people shall drink. And Mosheh did so before the sons of Israel") — **LXX ADDITION: ὁ λαός μου ("my people")** — the LXX adds the possessive "my," making explicit YHWH's claim on the people; the Hebrew has simply ha-am ("the people"). Also note the LXX reads "before the sons of Israel" rather than "before the eyes of the elders of Yisra'el" — a notable difference that broadens the witness group.

**7** And he called [vayiqra] the name [shem] of the place [ha-maqom] Massah [Testing] and Merivah [Quarrel], upon [al] the riv [quarrel] of the sons of Yisra'el, and upon [ve-al] their testing [nassotam] YHWH, saying [lemor]: "Is [ha-yesh] YHWH in our midst [be-qirbenu] or not [im-ayin]?"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּקְרָא שֵׁם הַמָּקוֹם מַסָּה וּמְרִיבָה עַל־רִיב בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְעַל נַסֹּתָם אֶת־יְהוָה לֵאמֹר הֲיֵשׁ יְהוָה בְּקִרְבֵּנוּ אִם־אָיִן (vayiqra shem ha-maqom Massah u-Merivah al-riv benei Yisra'el ve-al nassotam et-YHWH lemor ha-yesh YHWH be-qirbenu im-ayin, "and he called the name of the place Massah and Merivah, upon the quarrel of the sons of Yisra'el, and upon their testing YHWH, saying: 'Is YHWH in our midst or not?'"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the double naming and the fundamental question:** מַסָּה (Massah) = "testing, trial, proof" — from נסה (nassah, "to test, try, prove"). The place is named for the people's act of putting YHWH on trial. מְרִיבָה (Merivah) = "quarrel, contention, lawsuit" — from ריב (riv, "to quarrel, contend at law"). The place bears the double name of Yisra'el's sin: they tested and quarreled. These names become permanent markers in Israel's memory (cf. Deut 6:16; 9:22; 33:8; Ps 95:8; 106:32). נַסֹּתָם (nassotam) = piel infinitive construct of נסה (nassah) + 3mp suffix — "their testing." The fundamental question: הֲיֵשׁ יְהוָה בְּקִרְבֵּנוּ אִם־אָיִן (ha-yesh YHWH be-qirbenu im-ayin) = "Is YHWH in our midst or not?" — this is the crisis beneath the crisis. The water shortage is not merely physical but theological: the people doubt YHWH's presence among them. The word בְּקִרְבֵּנוּ (be-qirbenu) = "in our midst, in our inner part" — from קֶרֶב (qerev, "midst, inner part, entrails"), from קרב (qarav, "to draw near"). אָיִן (ayin) = "nothing, non-existence" — the alternative to YHWH's presence is void, absence, nothingness. The question "Is YHWH among us or ayin?" is the wilderness question par excellence — and Psalm 95:7–11 warns later generations not to repeat it. LXX: καὶ ἐπωνόμασεν τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ τόπου ἐκείνου Πειρασμὸς καὶ Λοιδόρησις διὰ τὴν λοιδορίαν τῶν υἱῶν Ισραηλ καὶ διὰ τὸ πειράζειν κύριον λέγοντας· εἰ ἔστιν κύριος ἐν ἡμῖν ἢ οὔ; ("and he named the name of that place 'Testing' and 'Reviling,' because of the reviling of the sons of Israel and because of their testing the Lord, saying: 'Is the Lord among us or not?'") — Πειρασμός ("testing, temptation") for Massah; Λοιδόρησις ("reviling, reproach") for Merivah — the LXX again renders riv as "reviling" rather than "lawsuit/quarrel."

---

## The Battle with Amaleq (vv. 8–16)

**8** And Amaleq came [vayavo] and he fought [vayillakhem] with Yisra'el at Refidim.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיָּבֹא עֲמָלֵק וַיִּלָּחֶם עִם־יִשְׂרָאֵל בִּרְפִידִם (vayavo Amaleq vayillakhem im-Yisra'el bi-Rfidim, "and Amaleq came and fought with Yisra'el at Refidim"). **Key terms:** עֲמָלֵק (Amaleq) = the eponymous ancestor and people descended from Esau (cf. Gen 36:12, 16 — Amaleq was the son of Elifaz, Esau's firstborn, by his concubine Timna). The Amaleqites inhabited the Negev and Sinai regions. Their attack on Yisra'el in the wilderness becomes paradigmatic of enmity against God's people. Deuteronomy 25:17–19 recalls this attack and commands Yisra'el to "blot out the memory of Amaleq from under the heavens." וַיִּלָּחֶם (vayillakhem) = niphal of לחם (lakham, "to fight, wage war") — "he fought." The root לחם is the same as לֶחֶם (lekhem, "bread, food") — some scholars note the semantic overlap between fighting and consuming, though the connection is debated. The attack comes without provocation and without warning — the narrative gives no cause, no embassy, no negotiation. Amaleq simply comes and fights. LXX: καὶ ἦλθεν Αμαληκ καὶ ἐπολέμει Ισραηλ ἐν Ραφιδιν ("and Amalek came and was waging war against Israel at Raphidin") — πολεμέω ("wage war, fight") for vayillakhem.

**9** And Mosheh said to Yehoshua: "Choose [bekhar] for us men [anashim], and go out [ve-tse], fight [hillakhem] against Amaleq. Tomorrow [makhar] I [am] standing [nitsav] upon the top [rosh] of the hill [ha-giv'ah], and the matteh [staff] of ha-Elohim [God] [is] in my hand [be-yadi]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־יְהוֹשֻׁעַ בְּחַר־לָנוּ אֲנָשִׁים וְצֵא הִלָּחֵם בַּעֲמָלֵק מָחָר אָנֹכִי נִצָּב עַל־רֹאשׁ הַגִּבְעָה וּמַטֵּה הָאֱלֹהִים בְּיָדִי (vayomer Mosheh el-Yehoshua bekhar-lanu anashim ve-tse hillakhem ba-Amaleq makhar anokhi nitsav al-rosh ha-giv'ah u-matteh ha-Elohim be-yadi, "and Mosheh said to Yehoshua: 'Choose for us men, and go out, fight against Amaleq. Tomorrow I [am] standing upon the top of the hill, and the staff of God [is] in my hand'"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the first appearance of Yehoshua and the matteh ha-Elohim:** יְהוֹשֻׁעַ (Yehoshua) = "YHWH saves / YHWH is salvation" — from יהוה (YHWH) + ישׁע (yasha, "to save, deliver"). This is the first mention of Yehoshua bin Nun in the Torah. He appears without introduction, without genealogy, without backstory — he is simply given a military command by Mosheh and obeys. He will become Mosheh's attendant (mesharet, 24:13; 33:11), one of the twelve spies (Num 13:8, 16), and ultimately Mosheh's successor to lead Yisra'el into Kena'an (Deut 31:7–8, 23; Josh 1:1–9). בְּחַר (bekhar) = qal imperative of בחר (bakhar, "to choose, select") — "choose!" The selection of warriors is Yehoshua's task. גִּבְעָה (giv'ah) = "hill, height" — from גבע (give'a, "to be high, rounded"). Mosheh will stand on the hilltop, not in the valley where the fighting occurs. His role is not military but intercessory. מַטֵּה הָאֱלֹהִים (matteh ha-Elohim) = "the staff of God" — the same staff called "the staff of Mosheh" (4:20) and "the staff of Aharon" (7:9), now explicitly named "the staff of God." It is the instrument through which divine power flows. נִצָּב (nitsav) = niphal participle of יצב (yatsav, "to station oneself, take one's stand") — "standing, stationed." Mosheh's posture is deliberate and fixed — he takes his stand. LXX: εἶπεν δὲ Μωυσῆς τῷ Ἰησοῦ· ἐπίλεξον σεαυτῷ ἄνδρας δυνατοὺς καὶ ἐξελθὼν παράταξαι τῷ Αμαληκ αὔριον· καὶ ἰδοὺ ἐγὼ ἕστηκα ἐπὶ τῆς κορυφῆς τοῦ βουνοῦ, καὶ ἡ ῥάβδος τοῦ θεοῦ ἐν τῇ χειρί μου ("and Mosheh said to Iesous: 'Select for yourself mighty men, and going out, draw up in battle against Amalek tomorrow; and behold, I stand upon the top of the hill, and the rod of God [is] in my hand'") — **LXX ADDITION: δυνατούς ("mighty, powerful")** — the LXX adds "mighty" to describe the men, not present in the Hebrew. Ἰησοῦς (Iesous) is the Greek form of Yehoshua — the same name later borne by Jesus of Nazareth. κορυφή ("summit, top") for rosh; βουνός ("hill") for giv'ah.

**10** And Yehoshua did as Mosheh said [amar] to him, to fight [le-hillakhem] against Amaleq; and Mosheh, Aharon, and Khur [Hur] went up [alu] [to the] top [rosh] of the hill [ha-giv'ah].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּעַשׂ יְהוֹשֻׁעַ כַּאֲשֶׁר אָמַר־לוֹ מֹשֶׁה לְהִלָּחֵם בַּעֲמָלֵק וּמֹשֶׁה אַהֲרֹן וְחוּר עָלוּ רֹאשׁ הַגִּבְעָה (vaya'as Yehoshua ka-asher amar-lo Mosheh le-hillakhem ba-Amaleq u-Mosheh Aharon ve-Khur alu rosh ha-giv'ah, "and Yehoshua did as Mosheh said to him, to fight against Amaleq; and Mosheh, Aharon, and Khur went up [to the] top of the hill"). **Key terms:** חוּר (Khur / Hur) = a figure who appears here without introduction, like Yehoshua. According to later tradition (cf. Josephus, Antiquities 3.2.4), Khur was the husband of Miriam and thus Mosheh's brother-in-law. In 24:14, Mosheh and Yehoshua leave Aharon and Khur in charge of the people during the Sinai ascent. The name חוּר may be related to חוּר/חֹר (khur/khor, "hole" or "white, noble"). Three men ascend the hill: Mosheh the intercessor with the staff, Aharon and Khur as supporters. The battle unfolds on two planes simultaneously — in the valley (Yehoshua and the warriors) and on the hilltop (Mosheh, Aharon, and Khur). LXX: καὶ ἐποίησεν Ἰησοῦς καθάπερ εἶπεν αὐτῷ Μωυσῆς, καὶ ἐξελθὼν παρετάξατο τῷ Αμαληκ· καὶ Μωυσῆς καὶ Ααρων καὶ Ωρ ἀνέβησαν ἐπὶ τὴν κορυφὴν τοῦ βουνοῦ ("and Iesous did just as Mosheh said to him, and going out drew up in battle against Amalek; and Mosheh and Aaron and Or went up to the top of the hill") — Ωρ (Or) is the LXX rendering of Khur.

**11** And it was [ve-hayah] when [ka-asher] Mosheh raised [yarim] his hand [yado], Yisra'el prevailed [ve-gavar]; and when [u-kha-asher] he rested [yanikah] his hand [yado], Amaleq prevailed [ve-gavar].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהָיָה כַּאֲשֶׁר יָרִים מֹשֶׁה יָדוֹ וְגָבַר יִשְׂרָאֵל וְכַאֲשֶׁר יָנִיחַ יָדוֹ וְגָבַר עֲמָלֵק (ve-hayah ka-asher yarim Mosheh yado ve-gavar Yisra'el ve-kha-asher yanikah yado ve-gavar Amaleq, "and it was — when Mosheh raised his hand, Yisra'el prevailed; and when he rested his hand, Amaleq prevailed"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the raised hands of Mosheh:** The verb יָרִים (yarim) = hiphil imperfect of רום (rum, "to be high, be exalted, lift up") — "he would raise, he raised." The verb יָנִיחַ (yanikah) = hiphil imperfect of נוח (nuakh, "to rest, settle, set down") — "he would rest, he lowered." The pattern is mechanical and direct: hand up = Yisra'el wins; hand down = Amaleq wins. This is not magic but theology — the raised hand holding the matteh ha-Elohim is the posture of intercessory appeal to YHWH. The Mishnah (Rosh Hashanah 3:8) asks: "Did the hands of Mosheh make war or break war? Rather, when Yisra'el looked upward and subjected their hearts to their Father in heaven, they prevailed; and when they did not, they fell." The singular יָדוֹ (yado, "his hand") is notable — the MT reads "hand" (singular), though v. 12 shifts to "hands" (plural, yadav). Some scholars suggest the singular refers to the hand holding the staff. וְגָבַר (ve-gavar) = qal of גבר (gavar, "to be strong, prevail, be mighty") — from the same root as גִּבּוֹר (gibbor, "mighty man, warrior"). The verb is used identically for both sides: whoever prevails, prevails by the same verb — the only variable is Mosheh's hand. LXX: καὶ ἐγίνετο ὅταν ἐπῆρεν Μωυσῆς τὰς χεῖρας, κατίσχυεν Ισραηλ· ὅταν δὲ καθῆκεν τὰς χεῖρας, κατίσχυεν Αμαληκ ("and it came to be that when Mosheh raised his hands, Israel prevailed; and when he let down his hands, Amalek prevailed") — the LXX reads τὰς χεῖρας ("the hands," plural) throughout, harmonizing with v. 12, where the MT itself uses the plural.

**12** And the hands [vi-dei] of Mosheh [were] heavy [kvedim], and they took [vayiqkhu] a stone [even] and they placed [vayasimu] under him [takhtav], and he sat [vayeshev] upon it [aleha]; and Aharon and Khur supported [tamekhu] his hands [be-yadav] — one [ekhad] from this side [mi-zeh] and one [ekhad] from this side [mi-zeh]; and his hands [yadav] were [vayehi] steadfast [emunah] until the going in [ad-bo] of the sun [ha-shemesh].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וִידֵי מֹשֶׁה כְּבֵדִים וַיִּקְחוּ אֶבֶן וַיָּשִׂימוּ תַחְתָּיו וַיֵּשֶׁב עָלֶיהָ וְאַהֲרֹן וְחוּר תָּמְכוּ בְיָדָיו מִזֶּה אֶחָד וּמִזֶּה אֶחָד וַיְהִי יָדָיו אֱמוּנָה עַד־בֹּא הַשָּׁמֶשׁ (vi-dei Mosheh kvedim vayiqkhu even vayasimu takhtav vayeshev aleha ve-Aharon ve-Khur tamekhu ve-yadav mi-zeh ekhad u-mi-zeh ekhad vayehi yadav emunah ad-bo ha-shemesh, "and the hands of Mosheh [were] heavy, and they took a stone and placed [it] under him, and he sat upon it; and Aharon and Khur supported his hands — one from this [side] and one from this [side]; and his hands were steadfast until the going in of the sun"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — kvedim, emunah, and the theology of supported hands:** כְּבֵדִים (kvedim) = "heavy" — plural adjective from כבד (kaved, "to be heavy"). The same root as כָּבוֹד (kavod, "glory, heaviness"). Mosheh's hands are heavy with the weight of intercession — the word for "heavy" is cognate with the word for "glory." The heaviness is not weakness but intensity. אֶבֶן (even) = "stone" — they provide a stone as a seat. Mosheh cannot stand indefinitely; the battle lasts all day. תָּמְכוּ (tamekhu) = qal of תמך (tamakh, "to support, uphold, sustain") — "they supported, upheld." Aharon and Khur physically hold up Mosheh's arms. Intercession is not a solitary act — it requires community support. אֱמוּנָה (emunah) = "steadfastness, faithfulness, firmness" — from אמן (aman, "to be firm, be established, be faithful"). This is one of the most theologically loaded words in the Hebrew Bible. Emunah is the quality of YHWH's covenant faithfulness (cf. Deut 32:4; Ps 36:6; 89:2; Lam 3:23) and the quality of human trust in YHWH (cf. Hab 2:4 — "the righteous shall live by his emunah"). Here it describes the physical state of Mosheh's hands: they were emunah — steady, firm, unwavering. The word choice is deliberate: the hands that intercede must be faithful/steady, and their steadiness is itself an act of emunah (faith/faithfulness). עַד־בֹּא הַשָּׁמֶשׁ (ad-bo ha-shemesh) = "until the going in of the sun" = "until sunset." The battle lasts the entire day, from morning until evening. LXX: αἱ δὲ χεῖρες Μωυσῆ βαρεῖαι· καὶ λαβόντες λίθον ὑπέθηκαν ὑπ᾽ αὐτόν, καὶ ἐκάθητο ἐπ᾽ αὐτοῦ· καὶ Ααρων καὶ Ωρ ἐστήριζον τὰς χεῖρας αὐτοῦ, ἐντεῦθεν εἷς καὶ ἐντεῦθεν εἷς· καὶ ἐγένοντο αἱ χεῖρες Μωυσῆ ἐστηριγμέναι ἕως δυσμῶν ἡλίου ("and the hands of Mosheh [were] heavy; and taking a stone they placed [it] under him, and he sat upon it; and Aaron and Or were supporting his hands, one on this side and one on this side; and the hands of Mosheh became supported until the setting of the sun") — βαρύς ("heavy") for kaved; στηρίζω ("support, make firm") for tamakh; ἐστηριγμέναι ("made firm, supported") for emunah — the LXX renders the abstract noun emunah as a passive participle ("supported/firm"), losing the theological resonance of "faithfulness."

**13** And Yehoshua weakened [vayakhalosh] Amaleq and his people [ve-et-ammo] by the mouth [lefi] of the kherev [sword].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּחֲלֹשׁ יְהוֹשֻׁעַ אֶת־עֲמָלֵק וְאֶת־עַמּוֹ לְפִי־חָרֶב (vayakhalosh Yehoshua et-Amaleq ve-et-ammo lefi-kharev, "and Yehoshua weakened Amaleq and his people by the mouth of the sword"). **Key terms:** וַיַּחֲלֹשׁ (vayakhalosh) = qal (or possibly hiphil) of חלשׁ (khalash, "to be weak, to weaken, to prostrate") — "he weakened, he disabled, he prostrated." This verb occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible — a hapax legomenon. The choice of khalash rather than the more common נכה (nakhah, "to strike") or הרג (harag, "to kill") is notable: Yehoshua weakened Amaleq, not annihilated him. The victory is decisive but not total — Amaleq will return (cf. 1 Sam 15; 30). לְפִי־חָרֶב (lefi-kharev) = "by/at the mouth of the sword" — from פֶּה (peh, "mouth") + חֶרֶב (kherev, "sword"). The sword has a "mouth" — a devouring edge. This is a common Hebrew idiom for military slaughter (cf. Gen 34:26; Deut 13:15; Josh 6:21; 8:24; 10:28). LXX: καὶ ἐτρέψατο Ἰησοῦς τὸν Αμαληκ καὶ πάντα τὸν λαὸν αὐτοῦ ἐν φόνῳ μαχαίρας ("and Iesous routed Amalek and all his people with slaughter of the sword") — τρέπω ("turn, rout") for vayakhalosh; φόνος μαχαίρας ("slaughter of the sword") for lefi-kharev.

**14** And YHWH said to Mosheh: "Write [ketov] this [zot] [as] a memorial [zikkaron] in the sefer [scroll/book], and place [ve-sim] [it] in the ears [be-oznei] of Yehoshua, that I will utterly blot out [makhoh emkheh] the remembrance [zekher] of Amaleq from under [mi-takhat] the shamayim [heavens]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה כְּתֹב זֹאת זִכָּרוֹן בַּסֵּפֶר וְשִׂים בְּאָזְנֵי יְהוֹשֻׁעַ כִּי־מָחֹה אֶמְחֶה אֶת־זֵכֶר עֲמָלֵק מִתַּחַת הַשָּׁמָיִם (vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh ketov zot zikkaron ba-sefer ve-sim be-oznei Yehoshua ki-makhoh emkheh et-zekher Amaleq mi-takhat ha-shamayim, "and YHWH said to Mosheh: 'Write this [as] a memorial in the scroll, and place [it] in the ears of Yehoshua, that utterly blotting out I will blot out the remembrance of Amaleq from under the heavens'"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — writing, memory, and the divine oath of obliteration:** כְּתֹב (ketov) = qal imperative of כתב (katav, "to write") — "write!" This is the first explicit divine command to write in the Torah. Writing becomes the vehicle of memorial — the event is to be preserved in a sefer (scroll, book, document). סֵפֶר (sefer) = "scroll, book, document" — from ספר (safar, "to count, recount, tell"). The definite article בַּסֵּפֶר (ba-sefer, "in the scroll/book") may indicate a specific document already in use, possibly the early form of the Torah narrative itself. זִכָּרוֹן (zikkaron) = "memorial, remembrance" — from זכר (zakhar, "to remember"). The irony is devastating: the memorial (zikkaron) of this event is written so that the remembrance (zekher) of Amaleq will be blotted out. Memory is used to destroy memory. בְּאָזְנֵי (be-oznei) = "in the ears of" — from אֹזֶן (ozen, "ear"). Mosheh must recite it aloud to Yehoshua — the written memorial must also be spoken and heard. מָחֹה אֶמְחֶה (makhoh emkheh) = infinitive absolute + imperfect of מחה (makhah, "to blot out, wipe away, obliterate") — "utterly blotting out I will blot out." The infinitive absolute construction intensifies: this is certain, total obliteration. זֵכֶר (zekher) = "remembrance, memory, name" — from זכר (zakhar, "to remember"). Not just the people but their very memory is to be erased. This is the most extreme form of divine judgment: not just death but extinction from historical memory. The command is specifically directed to Yehoshua — he will lead the next generation and must carry this divine mandate. LXX: εἶπεν δὲ κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν· κατάγραψον τοῦτο εἰς μνημόσυνον ἐν βιβλίῳ καὶ δὸς εἰς τὰ ὦτα Ἰησοῦ, ὅτι ἀλοιφῇ ἐξαλείψω τὸ μνημόσυνον Αμαληκ ἐκ τῆς ὑπὸ τὸν οὐρανόν ("and the Lord said to Mosheh: 'Write this down as a memorial in a book, and give [it] into the ears of Iesous, that with obliteration I will obliterate the memorial of Amalek from under heaven'") — μνημόσυνον ("memorial") for both zikkaron and zekher; βιβλίον ("book, scroll") for sefer; ἐξαλείφω ("wipe out, obliterate") for makhah.

**15** And Mosheh built [vayiven] an altar [mizbe'akh], and he called [vayiqra] its name [shemo] "YHWH Nissi" [YHWH is my banner].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּבֶן מֹשֶׁה מִזְבֵּחַ וַיִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ יְהוָה נִסִּי (vayiven Mosheh mizbe'akh vayiqra shemo YHWH Nissi, "and Mosheh built an altar, and he called its name 'YHWH Nissi'"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the altar-name YHWH Nissi:** מִזְבֵּחַ (mizbe'akh) = "altar" — from זבח (zavakh, "to slaughter, sacrifice"). The altar is not described as a place of sacrifice here but as a monument, a memorial marker. Its purpose is its name. יְהוָה נִסִּי (YHWH Nissi) = "YHWH [is] my banner / YHWH [is] my standard" — from נֵס (nes, "banner, standard, signal, ensign, pole"). The nes is the rallying point in battle — the flag or standard around which warriors gather and by which they orient themselves. To say "YHWH is my nes" is to say: YHWH is the one around whom we rally; YHWH is the signal that directs the battle; YHWH is the standard under which we fight. The nes is also related to the verb נסס (nasas, "to be lifted up, to be conspicuous") and possibly to נסה (nassah, "to test") — though the etymological connections are debated. The naming of altars after divine attributes is a recurring pattern: cf. Gen 33:20 (El-Elohei-Yisra'el, "El, the God of Israel"); Judg 6:24 (YHWH Shalom, "YHWH [is] Peace"). The altar stands as a permanent theological declaration: this victory belonged to YHWH. LXX: καὶ ᾠκοδόμησεν Μωυσῆς θυσιαστήριον κυρίῳ καὶ ἐπωνόμασεν τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ Κύριος μου καταφυγή ("and Mosheh built an altar to the Lord, and named its name 'The Lord is my refuge'") — **LXX INTERPRETIVE SHIFT:** The LXX renders נִסִּי (Nissi, "my banner") as καταφυγή μου (kataphyge mou, "my refuge") — a significant theological shift from military rallying-standard to protective shelter. The LXX translators interpreted nes not as "banner" but through the related sense of "refuge, place of escape" (cf. Isa 11:10 where nes is rendered as a rallying point for the nations).

**16** And he said: "For a hand [ki-yad] upon the throne [kes] of Yah [YHWH] — milkhamah [war] for YHWH against Amaleq from generation [mi-dor] to generation [dor]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר כִּי־יָד עַל־כֵּס יָהּ מִלְחָמָה לַיהוָה בַּעֲמָלֵק מִדֹּר דֹּר (vayomer ki-yad al-kes Yah milkhamah la-YHWH ba-Amaleq mi-dor dor, "and he said: 'For a hand upon the throne of Yah — war for YHWH against Amaleq from generation to generation'"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the most cryptic verse in the chapter:** This verse is notoriously difficult and has generated extensive rabbinic and scholarly discussion. כִּי־יָד עַל־כֵּס יָהּ (ki-yad al-kes Yah) = "for a hand upon the throne of Yah." The phrase is compressed and elliptical. Several interpretations exist: (a) "For a hand is upon/against the throne of Yah" — Amaleq has raised a hand against YHWH's throne, i.e., Amaleq's attack on Yisra'el is an assault on YHWH's sovereignty. (b) "For a hand [is raised] upon the throne of Yah" — a hand is placed on YHWH's throne as an oath-taking gesture, meaning YHWH swears by His own throne. (c) "For [there is] a hand upon the kes of Yah" — the kes (throne/seat) of Yah is the rallying point. The word כֵּס (kes) is unusual — the normal form is כִּסֵּא (kisse, "throne"). The truncated form kes occurs only here. Rabbinic tradition (Rashi, citing Midrash Tanchuma) explains: "The hand [of God] is raised upon the throne of the Holy One, blessed be He, to swear that He will have war and enmity against Amaleq forever. And why does it say kes rather than kisse? And [why] the name Yah rather than the full name YHWH? The Holy One, blessed be He, swore that His Name is not complete and His throne is not complete until the name of Amaleq is utterly blotted out." That is: the abbreviated forms kes (for kisse) and Yah (for YHWH) signal incompleteness — as long as Amaleq exists, YHWH's name and throne remain incomplete. יָהּ (Yah) = the short form of the divine name YHWH, used primarily in poetry and exclamations (cf. הַלְלוּ יָהּ, halelu-Yah, "praise Yah!"; Ex 15:2). מִלְחָמָה (milkhamah) = "war, battle" — from לחם (lakham, "to fight"). מִדֹּר דֹּר (mi-dor dor) = "from generation [to] generation" — an expression of perpetuity. The war against Amaleq is not a single battle but an ongoing, multigenerational divine commitment. This verse becomes the theological foundation for the command in Deuteronomy 25:17–19 to remember what Amaleq did and to blot out his name. In 1 Samuel 15, Sha'ul (Saul) is given the task of fulfilling this mandate and fails. LXX: καὶ εἶπεν· ὅτι ἐν χειρὶ κρυφαίᾳ πολεμεῖ κύριος ἐπὶ Αμαληκ ἀπὸ γενεῶν εἰς γενεάς ("and he said: 'For with a hidden hand the Lord wages war against Amalek from generations to generations'") — **LXX INTERPRETIVE SHIFT:** The LXX renders the cryptic כֵּס יָהּ (kes Yah, "throne of Yah") as χειρὶ κρυφαίᾳ (kheiri kryphaiai, "hidden hand") — a completely different reading. The translators apparently read or interpreted kes differently, perhaps connecting it to כסה (kasah, "to cover, conceal"), yielding "hidden/secret." The result is a strikingly different theology: YHWH wages war against Amaleq with a hidden hand — covertly, behind the scenes, through the working of providence across history.

---

## Theological Summary

Exodus 17 presents two crises that together frame the central question of Yisra'el's wilderness existence: Is YHWH present and active among His people?

Key theological threads:

- **Ha-yesh YHWH be-qirbenu im-ayin — "Is YHWH in our midst or not?" (v. 7):** This is the theological heart of the first episode. The water crisis is not merely about hydration but about the presence of God. Thirst becomes a test case for divine faithfulness. The people riv (bring a legal case) against Mosheh and nassah (put on trial) YHWH — they demand that YHWH prove Himself. The place names Massah ("Testing") and Merivah ("Quarrel") memorialize this sin for all generations.

- **YHWH standing upon the rock (v. 6):** The phrase hineni omed lefanekha sham al-ha-tsur is extraordinary — YHWH Himself stands on the rock. When Mosheh strikes, he strikes where God stands, and water flows from beneath God's feet. The tsur (rock) is both geological reality and divine metaphor — YHWH is the Rock of Yisra'el (Deut 32:4). The water flows not from stone but from the presence of God.

- **The matteh that struck the Ye'or (v. 5):** The staff that turned the Nile to blood now draws water from rock. The instrument of judgment becomes the instrument of provision. What destroyed water in Egypt creates water in the wilderness. The same divine power operates in both — it is context, not the tool, that determines whether God's act brings plague or sustenance.

- **Yehoshua — YHWH saves (v. 9):** Yehoshua appears without introduction, already trusted with military command. His name (YHWH saves) encodes the theology of the battle: it is not Yehoshua's sword but YHWH's salvation that wins the day. The man named "YHWH saves" fights in the valley while the man whose raised hands invoke YHWH's power stands on the hilltop.

- **The raised hands and emunah (vv. 11–12):** Mosheh's raised hands determine the battle's outcome — hands up, Yisra'el prevails; hands down, Amaleq prevails. The hands become emunah ("steadfast, faithful") through the support of Aharon and Khur. This is the Bible's most vivid image of intercession: faithfulness is embodied, physical, exhausting, and communal. No one can hold their hands up forever alone.

- **YHWH Nissi — "YHWH is my banner" (v. 15):** The altar-name declares that YHWH Himself is the military standard, the rallying point, the flag under which Yisra'el fights. Victory belongs not to the warriors but to the God who is both banner and battle-lord.

- **Ki-yad al-kes Yah — the incomplete throne (v. 16):** The chapter's final verse is its most enigmatic. The abbreviated forms kes (for kisse, "throne") and Yah (for YHWH) signal, according to rabbinic tradition, that God's name and sovereignty remain incomplete as long as Amaleq endures. The war is milkhamah la-YHWH — war belonging to YHWH, war on YHWH's behalf — from generation to generation, without end until the final obliteration of the enemy of God's people.
